standard.title: Interactive Connectivity Establishment (ICE): A Protocol for Network Address Translator (NAT) Traversal

summary.applicability_rationale: ICE is implemented by the browser's WebRTC stack, not by application code. However, the application's configuration choices (ICE server list, lack of TURN servers, no ICE restart handling, candidate buffering logic) directly affect whether the browser can achieve full ICE compliance and successful connectivity across network topologies.

findings.requirement: An ICE agent gathers candidates when communication is imminent, including host, server-reflexive, peer-reflexive, and relayed candidates.

evidence.observation: The application configures only two Google public STUN servers. No TURN servers are configured. This means the browser can only gather host candidates and server-reflexive candidates. Relayed candidates (which require TURN) cannot be gathered. Per Section 5.1.1.2, an ICE agent SHOULD gather server-reflexive and relayed candidates. The STUN servers enable server-reflexive gathering, but relayed gathering is impossible.
evidence.gap_description: No TURN server is configured, preventing the browser from gathering relayed candidates. In symmetric NAT scenarios or restrictive firewall environments, connections will fail because only server-reflexive and host candidates are available.

findings.remediation: Add at least one TURN server to the iceServers configuration. Example: { urls: 'turn:turn.example.com:3478', username: 'user', credential: 'pass' }. Consider using a TURN service provider such as Twilio, Xirsys, or self-hosted coturn.

findings.requirement: An ICE agent SHOULD gather server-reflexive and relayed candidates. If an agent does not gather relayed candidates, it is RECOMMENDED that the functionality be implemented and just disabled through configuration.

evidence.observation: No TURN server credentials or URLs are present in the configuration. There is no mechanism in the application to configure or enable TURN. The RFC recommends that even if TURN is not used, the functionality should be implemented and configurable.
evidence.gap_description: TURN relay functionality is neither configured nor configurable. Users behind symmetric NATs, strict firewalls, or carrier-grade NATs will be unable to establish media connections.

findings.remediation: Add TURN server configuration with credentials. Consider making TURN server configuration available through environment variables or a configuration file. At minimum, provide a fallback TURN relay for cases where direct connectivity fails.

findings.requirement: Once ICE is concluded, it can be restarted at any time for one or all data streams by either ICE agent. This is done by sending updated candidate information indicating a restart.

evidence.observation: When a connection enters 'disconnected' or 'failed' state, the application simply removes the peer entirely rather than attempting an ICE restart. The RTCPeerConnection API supports restartIce() for this purpose, but the application does not use it. No iceconnectionstatechange handler or iceRestart logic exists.
evidence.gap_description: The application has no ICE restart capability. Network changes (e.g., switching from Wi-Fi to cellular, temporary network disruption) will cause permanent peer disconnection instead of attempting reconnection via ICE restart.

findings.remediation: Implement ICE restart handling: when connectionState transitions to 'disconnected' or 'failed', call connection.restartIce() followed by creating a new offer with iceRestart: true, and re-negotiate via the signaling channel. Add exponential backoff for repeated failures.

findings.requirement: ICE agents need candidate information (address, transport, foundation, component ID, priority, type, related address) to be exchanged. Once an agent has sent its candidate information, it MUST be prepared to receive both STUN and data packets on each candidate.

evidence.observation: The application correctly relays ICE candidates via the signaling channel (Socket.IO). Each candidate generated by the browser is sent to the remote peer, and incoming candidates are properly added. The full RTCIceCandidate object is transmitted, which includes all required fields (address, port, type, foundation, priority, etc.) as serialized by the browser.

findings.standard_section: 5.3 Exchanging Candidate Information - Username Fragment and Password
findings.requirement: Username Fragment and Password values used to perform connectivity checks. The values MUST be unguessable, with at least 128 bits of random number generator output used to generate the password, and at least 24 bits of output to generate the username fragment.

evidence.observation: The browser's RTCPeerConnection implementation generates ICE username fragments and passwords automatically with cryptographically random values meeting the required entropy. The application does not override or interfere with these values. SDP offers/answers exchanged via signaling contain the browser-generated ufrag/pwd values.

findings.requirement: The initiating agent that started the ICE processing MUST take the controlling role, and the other MUST take the controlled role.

evidence.observation: The application correctly designates an initiator (the peer who creates and sends the offer) and a responder (the peer who receives the offer and sends an answer). The browser's ICE implementation handles role determination based on the offer/answer exchange. The offerer becomes the controlling agent and the answerer becomes the controlled agent.

findings.requirement: An ICE agent MUST send keepalives for each data session. The keepalives are sent using STUN Binding Indications. The default interval is 15 seconds.

evidence.observation: ICE keepalives are handled automatically by the browser's WebRTC implementation. The application does not disable or interfere with this behavior. Browsers send STUN Binding Indications at regular intervals (typically 15 seconds) to maintain NAT bindings.

findings.requirement: Each candidate for a data stream MUST have a unique priority that MUST be a positive integer between 1 and (2^31 - 1). The RECOMMENDED values for type preferences are 126 for host, 110 for peer-reflexive, 100 for server-reflexive, and 0 for relayed candidates.

evidence.observation: Candidate prioritization is handled entirely by the browser's WebRTC stack. The application does not override or modify candidate priorities. Browser implementations follow the recommended type preference values.

findings.requirement: The ICE agent processes connectivity check responses, handling role conflicts, failures (timeouts, ICMP errors), and successes including discovering peer-reflexive candidates and constructing valid pairs.

evidence.observation: Connectivity check processing is handled by the browser. The application correctly buffers candidates that arrive before the remote description is set, then flushes them once the remote description is available. This ensures no candidates are lost during the offer/answer exchange.

findings.requirement: ICE implementations where IP privacy issues can arise are RECOMMENDED to provide a programmatic or user interface that provides control over which network interfaces are used to generate candidates.

evidence.observation: The application does not provide any mechanism to restrict ICE candidate types or control IP address privacy. There is no option to use TURN-only mode (iceTransportPolicy: 'relay') which would hide the user's real IP addresses from peers. The RTCPeerConnection configuration does not set an iceTransportPolicy.
evidence.gap_description: Users' local and server-reflexive IP addresses are exposed to peers with no option to restrict this. No iceTransportPolicy is configured, defaulting to 'all' which reveals all candidate types.

findings.remediation: Add an option for privacy-conscious users to set iceTransportPolicy to 'relay' in the RTCPeerConnection configuration. This requires adding TURN server support first. Consider providing a UI toggle for 'enhanced privacy mode' that forces relay-only connections.

findings.requirement: An ICE agent MUST limit the total number of connectivity checks the agent performs across all checklists. The default limit is 100, but the value MUST be configurable.

evidence.observation: This requirement is for the ICE implementation itself (the browser), not the application. The browser's WebRTC stack enforces candidate pair limits internally. The application has no control over this parameter via the RTCPeerConnection API.

findings.requirement: The controlling agent nominates valid pairs from the valid list for each component. The nominated pairs become the selected pairs for data transmission.

evidence.observation: Pair nomination is handled entirely by the browser's ICE implementation. The application has no direct control over or visibility into the nomination process. This is a protocol-level requirement implemented by the browser.

findings.requirement: Once server-reflexive and relayed candidates are allocated, they MUST be kept alive until ICE processing has completed. Bindings MUST be kept alive by additional Binding requests to the server.

evidence.observation: Candidate keepalives are managed by the browser's ICE implementation. The application does not interfere with this process. The browser sends periodic STUN Binding requests to keep server-reflexive candidate bindings alive at NATs.

findings.requirement: ICE agents SHOULD limit the total number of connectivity checks they perform to 100. Additionally, agents MAY limit the number of candidates they will accept.

evidence.observation: The application does not impose any limits on the number of ICE candidates it accepts from peers via signaling. However, the browser's WebRTC stack may impose its own limits internally. Whether the browser enforces the recommended 100-pair limit is browser-implementation-dependent and cannot be verified from application code alone.
evidence.gap_description: The application does not perform any server-side or client-side validation to limit the number of candidates a peer can send, which could theoretically be exploited for amplification if the browser does not enforce limits.

findings.remediation: Consider adding application-level limits on the number of ICE candidates accepted per peer (e.g., rejecting candidates beyond a reasonable threshold such as 50-100) as a defense-in-depth measure.

findings.requirement: ICE works with various NAT types. For symmetric NATs, relayed candidates (TURN) are typically required for connectivity.

evidence.observation: Without TURN servers, ICE cannot establish connectivity through symmetric NATs or restrictive firewalls. The operational considerations section notes that TURN is essential for ensuring connectivity across all NAT types. The application only configures STUN servers, limiting connectivity to scenarios where at least one peer has a non-symmetric NAT or a direct route.
evidence.gap_description: Users behind symmetric NATs, strict enterprise firewalls, or certain mobile carrier networks will be unable to connect. This is a significant interoperability issue for a public-facing conferencing application.

findings.remediation: Deploy a TURN server (e.g., coturn) and add its configuration to iceServers. For production use, consider a geo-distributed TURN infrastructure or a managed TURN service.

findings.requirement: An ICE agent MUST NOT send data to a candidate pair until it has been validated through connectivity checks and nominated.

evidence.observation: The browser's WebRTC implementation ensures that media data is only sent after ICE connectivity checks succeed and the DTLS handshake completes. The application adds tracks before connection establishment, which is the correct pattern - tracks are queued and media only flows after ICE/DTLS completes.

findings.requirement: It is RECOMMENDED that a domain name be configured, and DNS procedures be used to discover STUN/TURN servers via SRV records.

evidence.observation: STUN servers are hardcoded by IP-resolved hostname rather than being discovered via DNS SRV records. While using well-known Google STUN servers is common practice, it creates a dependency on a third-party service and does not follow the recommended DNS-based discovery pattern.
evidence.gap_description: STUN server addresses are hardcoded rather than being dynamically discovered through DNS SRV records as recommended.

findings.remediation: While hardcoded STUN servers are acceptable for most use cases, consider making the ICE server configuration dynamic (e.g., fetched from the signaling server at session start) to allow administrators to change servers without code changes.

findings.requirement: ICE can be restarted by either agent at any time. Both agents should handle transitions between ICE states properly.

evidence.observation: The application monitors connection state changes but only handles failure states by removing the peer. There is no handling of the 'disconnected' state as potentially transient (which it often is during network transitions). The 'disconnected' state should trigger an ICE restart attempt rather than immediate peer removal.
evidence.gap_description: The application treats both 'disconnected' and 'failed' states identically by removing the peer, but 'disconnected' is often transient and could be recovered via ICE restart. No reconnection logic exists.

findings.remediation: Differentiate between 'disconnected' and 'failed' states. For 'disconnected', implement a timeout (e.g., 5-10 seconds) before attempting ICE restart. For 'failed', attempt ICE restart before giving up. Only remove the peer after restart attempts are exhausted.

recommendations.title: Add TURN server support
recommendations.description: Deploy and configure at least one TURN server to enable relayed candidates. Without TURN, users behind symmetric NATs, strict firewalls, or carrier-grade NATs will be unable to establish peer connections. This is the single most impactful change for real-world connectivity reliability. Consider using coturn as a self-hosted solution or a managed service like Twilio Network Traversal Service or Xirsys.

recommendations.title: Implement ICE restart for connection recovery
recommendations.description: When a peer connection enters 'disconnected' or 'failed' state, attempt an ICE restart using RTCPeerConnection.restartIce() before removing the peer. This enables recovery from transient network changes such as Wi-Fi to cellular handoff, brief network interruptions, or NAT rebinding events. Implement exponential backoff for repeated failures.

recommendations.title: Add IP address privacy controls
recommendations.description: Provide an option for users to set iceTransportPolicy to 'relay' to hide their IP addresses from peers. This requires TURN server support. Consider adding a UI toggle for 'privacy mode' that forces relay-only connections at the cost of potentially higher latency.

recommendations.title: Make ICE server configuration dynamic
recommendations.description: Instead of hardcoding STUN server URLs in client JavaScript, fetch the ICE server configuration from the signaling server at session start. This allows administrators to change STUN/TURN servers, rotate TURN credentials, and implement per-session short-lived credentials without deploying client code changes.
